a: Before AI, this field was about automating calculation and logic — figuring out what can be computed, step by step, and how fast.
notes: Early computer science (1930s–50s) centered on computability and algorithms; 'learning' from data was not yet the goal.

a: Alan Turing's 1936 abstract machine that defined precisely what it means for a problem to be 'computable'.
notes: A tape + rules thought-experiment; it underpins all of computer science and framed what machines could, in principle, do.

a: Turing's 1950 proposal to sidestep that question by testing whether a machine's conversation is indistinguishable from a human's.
notes: From 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' (1950); it reframed 'intelligence' as observable behavior.

concept: Origins — The field gets a name
a: The 1956 summer workshop that coined 'artificial intelligence' and set the goal of making machines simulate intelligence.
notes: Organized by McCarthy, Minsky, Shannon and Rochester; the official birth of AI as a discipline.

concept: Origins — The first approach to AI
a: Early AI's dominant strategy: hand-encode human knowledge as explicit logic rules and symbols ('good old-fashioned AI').
notes: Dominated 1956–1980s; powerful for logic and games but brittle on messy, real-world perception.

a: Why exhaustively hand-listing rules for real-world tasks is hopeless: the cases and exceptions blow up past any hope of enumeration.
notes: The explosion of cases in rule-based systems is a core reason the field turned to learning rules from data.

a: Arthur Samuel's 1959 checkers program that improved by playing itself — an early, famous demonstration of a machine learning from experience.
notes: Samuel coined 'machine learning' (1959); the program got better with practice, no extra programming.

concept: Origins — The first practical ML task
a: The earliest big real-world ML problem: sorting inputs into categories, like reading handwritten digits or telling cats from dogs.
notes: Classification of perceptual data was the proving ground for early neural nets and remains a core ML task.

a: The room-sized 1940s electronic machine built to compute artillery firing tables — the icon of the computer as a fast, fixed calculator.
notes: ENIAC (1945) executed fixed arithmetic at speed; 'intelligence' wasn't on the menu — it did exactly what it was wired to do.

a: How early business computers ran: jobs like payroll and census were queued and executed in bulk with no interaction, following fixed instructions exactly.
notes: Business 'data processing' dominated; the computer was a meticulous clerk, not a learner.

a: The observation that tasks easy for humans — seeing, hearing, moving — are extremely hard for machines, while abstract reasoning is comparatively easy.
notes: Perception resists explicit rules; this paradox is part of why AI turned toward learning from data.

a: The slow, incomplete slog of extracting human experts' knowledge into hand-written rules — the wall that capped 1980s rule-based AI.
notes: This bottleneck motivated systems that infer their own rules from data instead of being hand-programmed.

a: The approach to AI in which intelligence emerges from networks of simple units that adjust their connection strengths — loosely imitating the brain.
notes: Connectionism gave neural networks their name and design; it rivalled symbolic AI and ultimately powered deep learning.

a: The broad field of building machines that perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence.
notes: Coined at the 1956 Dartmouth workshop; machine learning and deep learning are nested subfields within it.

a: Subfield where systems learn patterns from data instead of following hand-coded rules.
notes: Arthur Samuel popularized the term in 1959; the shift from explicit rules to learned parameters is its defining idea.

a: Machine learning using neural networks with many stacked layers that learn features automatically.
notes: 'Deep' refers to the number of layers; depth lets the model learn a hierarchy of features rather than relying on hand-engineering.

a: A model of interconnected nodes ('neurons') loosely inspired by the brain that maps inputs to outputs.
notes: Each connection has a weight; learning means adjusting those weights to reduce error.

a: The basic unit that sums weighted inputs, adds a bias, and applies an activation function.
notes: Also called a node or unit; stacking many of them in layers builds a network.

a: The process of feeding examples to a model and adjusting its weights to reduce error.
notes: Contrast with inference, where the trained model is used to make predictions.

a: Learning from labeled examples, mapping inputs to known correct outputs.
notes: The dominant paradigm for classification and regression; needs labeled data.

a: Finding structure in data that has no labels — grouping similar items together.
notes: Clustering (e.g. k-means) and dimensionality reduction are the classics; the model discovers patterns instead of predicting given answers.

a: Learning by trial and error: an agent takes actions in an environment to maximize reward.
notes: The agent observes a state, acts, and receives a reward; over time it learns a policy. This loop later became the backbone of RLHF for aligning LLMs.

a: The learnable numbers inside a network that get tuned during training.
notes: Modern LLMs have billions of these; their count is a rough proxy for model capacity.

a: The hardware breakthrough that made training large deep networks practical via massive parallelism.
notes: Originally for graphics, GPUs excel at the matrix math of neural nets; their use was key to the 2012 deep-learning boom.

a: The ImageNet-winning network that ignited the modern deep-learning era.
notes: Krizhevsky, Sutskever & Hinton's deep CNN crushed the 2012 ImageNet benchmark, proving deep nets + GPUs work at scale.

a: Using an already-trained model to make a prediction on new input.
notes: Distinct from training; most production cost in LLMs comes from inference at scale.

a: The collection of examples a model learns from.
notes: Quality, size and diversity of data often matter more than model tweaks.

a: Rosenblatt's 1958 single-layer learning device, the earliest trainable neural model.
notes: It could only learn linearly separable functions — a limit exposed in 1969 that stalled the field.

a: The nonlinearity applied to a neuron's weighted sum, letting networks model nonlinear relationships.
notes: Without it, stacked layers collapse into a single linear map; examples include sigmoid, tanh and ReLU.

a: The activation f(x)=max(0,x) that became the default in deep networks.
notes: Cheap to compute and avoids the saturation that plagued sigmoids, easing the vanishing-gradient problem.

a: The algorithm that computes gradients by propagating error backward through the layers.
notes: Popularized in 1986 by Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams; it makes training multilayer nets feasible.

a: Iteratively nudging weights in the direction that most reduces the loss.
notes: The workhorse optimizer; the gradient says which way is downhill on the loss surface.

a: The measurable objective the model tries to minimize during training.
notes: Examples: mean squared error for regression, cross-entropy for classification.

a: A layer between input and output whose activations are learned intermediate features.
notes: Adding hidden layers let networks solve nonlinear problems the perceptron could not.

a: A fully connected feedforward network with one or more hidden layers.
notes: Combined with backprop, the MLP overcame the single-perceptron's linear limitation.

a: When a model memorizes training data and fails to generalize to new data.
notes: Signaled by low training error but high validation error; combated with regularization and more data.

a: The learnable offset added to a neuron's weighted sum before activation.
notes: Lets the activation shift left or right, independent of the inputs.

a: One full pass of the training algorithm over the entire dataset.
notes: Training usually takes many epochs; too many can cause overfitting.

a: The step size controlling how far weights move on each gradient update.
notes: Too high diverges, too low crawls; arguably the most important hyperparameter.

a: The problem where gradients shrink toward zero in deep nets, stalling learning in early layers.
notes: Caused by repeatedly multiplying small derivatives (e.g. from sigmoids); it motivated ReLU, careful init and normalization.

a: Randomly zeroing a fraction of activations during training to prevent co-adaptation.
notes: Introduced by Srivastava & Hinton (2014); acts like training an ensemble and reduces overfitting.

a: The theorem stating a network with one hidden layer can approximate any continuous function.
notes: Proved by Cybenko (1989) and Hornik (1991); it justifies networks in principle but says nothing about learnability or efficiency.

a: The function that turns a vector of scores into a probability distribution over classes.
notes: Used at the output of classifiers and in attention; exponentiates and normalizes the logits.

a: The loss measuring the gap between predicted and true probability distributions.
notes: Standard for classification and language modeling; penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily.

a: Updating weights using small random mini-batches rather than the full dataset each step.
notes: Faster and noisier than full-batch descent; the noise can even help escape poor minima.

a: Why sigmoid/tanh units stop learning when their inputs are large in magnitude.
notes: Flat tails give near-zero gradients, feeding the vanishing-gradient problem — a key reason ReLU replaced them.

a: Techniques that constrain a model to improve generalization, e.g. weight penalties.
notes: L2 (weight decay) and L1 shrink weights; dropout and early stopping are also forms of it.

a: The tension between a model too simple to fit (high bias) and too flexible (high variance).
notes: Guides model complexity choices; deep nets complicate the classic picture (see double descent).

a: Why depth helps: each layer composes simpler features into more abstract ones.
notes: Early CNN layers learn edges, later ones learn objects — depth automates feature engineering.

a: The simple nonlinear function a single perceptron provably cannot learn.
notes: Minsky & Papert's 1969 'Perceptrons' highlighted this limit, helping trigger an AI winter until multilayer nets + backprop answered it.

a: The funding and interest collapse partly caused by the perceptron's exposed limitations.
notes: Minsky & Papert (1969) showed single-layer nets can't do XOR; enthusiasm only recovered with backprop-trained multilayer nets.

a: Normalizing layer inputs per mini-batch to stabilize and speed up training.
notes: Ioffe & Szegedy (2015) let much deeper nets train reliably by reducing sensitivity to initialization and learning rate.

a: The adaptive optimizer combining momentum with per-parameter learning rates.
notes: Kingma & Ba (2014); its robustness made it the default optimizer for most deep models.

a: When gradients grow uncontrollably large, destabilizing training (common in deep/recurrent nets).
notes: Mitigated by gradient clipping; the flip side of the vanishing-gradient issue.

a: The 1943 binary threshold logic unit that first modeled a neuron mathematically.
notes: Predates the perceptron; showed networks of simple units could compute logical functions.

a: The property of ReLU that keeps gradients alive for positive inputs, unlike saturating units.
notes: Constant gradient of 1 for x>0 lets error signals flow through deep stacks, directly addressing vanishing gradients.

a: Schemes like Xavier/He that set starting weights to keep signal variance stable across layers.
notes: Poor init causes vanishing or exploding activations; principled init was a prerequisite for training very deep nets.

a: Hinton's 2006 layer-wise pretrained nets that reignited interest in 'deep' learning.
notes: Greedy unsupervised pretraining (RBMs) first made deep nets trainable, before ReLU and big data made it unnecessary.

a: The general technique underlying backprop, predating its 1986 popularization.
notes: Linnainmaa described it in 1970 and Werbos applied it to neural nets in 1974; 'backprop' is its application to layered nets.

a: The core difficulty of deciding which weights deserve blame for an error across many layers/timesteps.
notes: Backprop solves it spatially; recurrent nets face a temporal version that LSTMs were designed to address.

a: The modern phenomenon where test error falls, rises, then falls again as model size grows past the interpolation point.
notes: Belkin et al. (2019) showed overparameterized nets can generalize well, complicating the classic bias–variance story.

a: The conjecture that dense nets contain sparse subnetworks that train to comparable accuracy in isolation.
notes: Frankle & Carbin (2018); reframes why overparameterization helps optimization.

a: The observation that adding layers to a plain deep net can increase training error, not just overfit.
notes: He et al. (2015) identified this and introduced residual connections (ResNet) as the fix.

a: The specific 1989 proof that sigmoidal one-hidden-layer nets are universal approximators.
notes: Hornik (1991) generalized it beyond sigmoids; both are existence results, silent on how to find the weights.

a: The diploma thesis that first formally analyzed why gradients vanish in deep recurrent nets.
notes: This analysis directly motivated the 1997 LSTM design with its constant error carousel.

a: Tishby's theory framing learning as compressing input while preserving output-relevant information.
notes: Offers one lens on why deep nets generalize; remains debated as an explanation of training dynamics.

a: The neural architecture specialized for grid data like images.
notes: Uses local filters and weight sharing to detect spatial patterns efficiently.

a: The architecture designed to process sequences by maintaining state across steps.
notes: Feeds its own previous output back in, giving it a form of memory for sequential data.

a: The small sliding window of weights a CNN convolves across an image.
notes: Each filter learns to detect one pattern, such as an edge or texture.

a: The CNN operation that downsamples feature maps to shrink them and add robustness.
notes: Max pooling keeps the strongest activation in each region, reducing size and adding small-shift tolerance.

a: The large labeled image dataset/benchmark that drove the deep-learning vision boom.
notes: AlexNet's 2012 win on it is widely seen as deep learning's breakout moment.

a: An RNN's running memory vector, updated at each timestep.
notes: It summarizes everything seen so far and is passed to the next step.

a: The gated recurrent network built to remember information over long sequences.
notes: Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997); its gates and cell state fight the vanishing-gradient problem.

a: A streamlined gated RNN with fewer gates than the LSTM.
notes: Cho et al. (2014); often matches LSTM performance with less computation.

a: Ordered data like text, audio or time series where order carries meaning.
notes: RNNs, LSTMs and GRUs were designed specifically to model it.

a: The output produced when a filter is convolved across an input.
notes: Stacking many feature maps lets a CNN detect many patterns in parallel.

a: The depth dimension of an image or feature map, e.g. the R, G and B of a color photo.
notes: Each convolutional filter spans all input channels and outputs one new channel.

a: What a CNN's earliest filters reliably learn to recognize.
notes: First-layer filters fire on edges and color blobs, the building blocks for later, complex features.

a: The operation of sliding a filter over input and computing dot products.
notes: Reuses the same weights across all positions, slashing parameters versus a dense layer.

a: The CNN principle of reusing one filter's weights across every spatial location.
notes: Drastically cuts parameters and builds in the assumption that a useful pattern is useful anywhere in the image.

a: Downsampling by taking the maximum value in each local region.
notes: Provides translation tolerance and reduces computation in deeper layers.

a: LeCun's pioneering CNN for handwritten digit recognition.
notes: The 1998 network demonstrated convolution+pooling+backprop on real images years before it scaled.

a: The 2012 deep CNN that won ImageNet and launched the deep-learning era.
notes: Used ReLU, dropout and two GPUs; its margin of victory convinced the field to go deep.

a: Backpropagation unrolled across an RNN's timesteps to compute gradients.
notes: Treats the unrolled sequence as a deep net; its depth in time is the source of vanishing gradients.

a: The feedback loop that feeds an RNN's output back as input for the next step.
notes: This is what gives RNNs memory but also makes them inherently sequential and slow to train.

a: The learned valves in an LSTM/GRU that control what information is kept or discarded.
notes: Sigmoid-controlled gates regulate the flow into and out of memory, enabling long-range retention.

a: The encoder–decoder design that maps one sequence to another, e.g. for translation.
notes: Sutskever et al. (2014); an encoder compresses the input, a decoder generates the output.

a: The 2014 CNN that showed depth via stacks of small 3×3 filters.
notes: Simonyan & Zisserman; simple and uniform, it popularized very deep, regular architectures.

a: The two settings that control how a filter steps across input and how borders are handled.
notes: Stride sets the step size (and downsampling); padding preserves spatial size at the edges.

a: Expanding training data with flips, crops and color shifts to fight overfitting.
notes: Cheap label-preserving transforms boosted CNN generalization and were central to AlexNet's win.

a: The CNN property that a learned pattern is recognized regardless of its position.
notes: Emerges from weight sharing plus pooling; it's why CNNs suit images so well.

a: The region of the input that influences a particular neuron's activation.
notes: Grows with depth; deep CNN units 'see' large parts of the image, enabling object-level features.

a: The challenge of connecting information separated by many timesteps in a sequence.
notes: Plain RNNs forget distant context as gradients vanish over time — the motivation for LSTMs and later attention.

a: The LSTM's protected memory line that carries information across many steps with minimal change.
notes: Gates add or remove information from it; this near-linear highway is what preserves gradients.

a: The LSTM gate that decides what to erase from the cell state.
notes: Added by Gers et al. (2000); learning to forget proved crucial for handling long sequences.

a: Why a GRU is often preferred despite the LSTM's longer track record.
notes: The GRU merges gates and drops the separate cell state, training faster with comparable accuracy.

a: An RNN that reads a sequence both forward and backward.
notes: Lets each position use both past and future context; common in tagging and pre-transformer NLP.

a: The two-part scheme where one network reads the input and another writes the output.
notes: Foundational to translation and later to transformers; the encoder's summary feeds the decoder.

a: The shortcut that adds a layer's input directly to its output.
notes: Introduced by ResNet (2015); it lets gradients flow past many layers, enabling 100+ layer nets.

a: Capping gradient magnitude to prevent the exploding-gradient instability in RNNs.
notes: A simple, standard fix that keeps recurrent training stable.

a: How ResNet enabled extremely deep networks despite the degradation problem.
notes: By learning F(x)+x, layers only need to learn a residual; identity shortcuts preserve gradients (He et al., 2015).

a: The flaw in early encoder–decoders that compress an entire input into one fixed vector.
notes: Long inputs overflow this single vector, hurting translation — the limitation attention was invented to solve.

a: The LSTM mechanism that lets error flow unchanged through the cell over many steps.
notes: The self-loop with weight 1 keeps gradients from vanishing — the conceptual heart of the LSTM.

concept: Vision & Sequence — Why CNNs Beat Dense Nets on Images
a: The two CNN properties that make them vastly more efficient than fully connected nets for pixels.
notes: They exploit spatial locality and reuse filters, cutting parameters by orders of magnitude and improving generalization.

a: Feeding the true previous token (not the model's guess) into the decoder during training.
notes: Speeds and stabilizes seq2seq training but can cause exposure bias at inference time.

concept: Vision & Sequence — Sequential Bottleneck of RNNs
a: The structural reason RNNs are slow to train on modern hardware.
notes: Each step depends on the previous one, so timesteps can't be parallelized — a key motivation for the Transformer.

a: The 2014 architecture using parallel multi-scale filters within a module.
notes: Processes features at several filter sizes at once, improving efficiency on ImageNet.

a: The task that fused a CNN encoder with an RNN decoder to describe images in words.
notes: Show-and-Tell (2015) used a CNN to encode the image and an LSTM to generate the caption — an early multimodal pipeline.

a: Fukushima's 1980 hierarchical vision model that prefigured the CNN.
notes: Introduced alternating feature-extraction and pooling layers; LeCun later added backprop to make it trainable.

a: The first application of backpropagation to a convolutional network (on ZIP-code digits).
notes: Demonstrated end-to-end learned convolutional features years before compute and data caught up.

a: He et al.'s 2016 refinement showing pre-activation residual blocks ease optimization further.
notes: Clean identity shortcuts let signal and gradient pass untransformed, enabling 1000-layer nets.

a: The 2015 gated-shortcut architecture that preceded ResNet's simpler additive shortcuts.
notes: Srivastava et al. used learned gates to carry information across depth; ResNet showed plain identity works.

a: An LSTM variant letting gates inspect the cell state directly.
notes: Gers & Schmidhuber (2000); improved precise timing tasks, illustrating LSTM gate engineering.

a: Convolutions with gaps that expand the receptive field without adding parameters.
notes: Used in WaveNet and segmentation to capture wide context cheaply.

a: The 2014 mechanism that let a decoder look back at all encoder states, breaking the bottleneck.
notes: This additive attention over the source sequence directly inspired the Transformer's self-attention three years later.

concept: Vision & Sequence — Vanishing Gradient in RNNs (Formal)
a: The reason error signals decay exponentially with sequence length in plain RNNs.
notes: Gradients are products of many recurrent Jacobians; eigenvalues <1 vanish, >1 explode — the CEC sidesteps this.

a: Representing a word as a dense vector of numbers that captures meaning.
notes: Similar words land near each other in the vector space, unlike arbitrary one-hot codes.

a: A database that stores embeddings and retrieves the nearest vectors to a query.
notes: Powers semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation for LLMs.

a: Searching by meaning rather than exact keyword match.
notes: Compares query and document embeddings, finding relevant results even with different wording.

a: The common measure of how close two vectors point in the same direction.
notes: Ranges from -1 to 1; widely used to compare embeddings regardless of their magnitude.

a: Splitting text into the small units a model actually processes.
notes: Tokens may be words or subwords; the model reads token IDs, not raw characters.

a: The list of numbers that encodes an item's meaning in a model.
notes: Text, images and audio can all be embedded into a shared numeric space.

a: Letting a model focus on the most relevant parts of the input when producing each output.
notes: Instead of one fixed summary, the model dynamically weighs all inputs per step.

a: The learned multi-dimensional space where embeddings live.
notes: Directions and distances in it correspond to semantic relationships.

a: The first layer of a language model that turns each token ID into its learned vector.
notes: A simple lookup table of vectors, trained jointly with the rest of the network.

a: A classic non-text use of embeddings: representing users and items as nearby vectors.
notes: Matrix-factorization vectors for users/items predate and parallel word embeddings.

a: Mikolov's 2013 method that learns word vectors from co-occurrence in large text.
notes: Trained by predicting context words; it popularized dense embeddings and famous analogy arithmetic.

a: The linguistic idea that words appearing in similar contexts have similar meanings.
notes: Firth's 'you shall know a word by the company it keeps' underpins all word embeddings.

a: Representing a word as a sparse vector with a single 1 and zeros elsewhere.
notes: Huge, sparse and meaningless about similarity — the limitation dense embeddings replaced.

a: The famous analogy that word vectors solve via arithmetic, yielding 'Queen'.
notes: Showed embeddings capture relational structure (gender, tense) as consistent directions in space.

a: Stanford's 2014 embeddings learned by factorizing a global co-occurrence matrix.
notes: Pennington et al.; an alternative to Word2Vec that uses corpus-wide statistics directly.

a: The three projected vectors at the heart of the attention mechanism.
notes: A query is matched against keys to weight the corresponding values — attention as soft lookup.

a: Information added to token embeddings so a transformer knows their order.
notes: Since self-attention is order-agnostic, position must be injected explicitly.

a: Finding the stored vectors closest to a query vector.
notes: The core operation of a vector database; done approximately at scale for speed.

a: The simplest similarity score between two vectors: multiply componentwise and sum.
notes: Cosine similarity is just the dot product of normalized vectors; both rank nearest matches.

a: Encoding pictures as vectors so visually or semantically similar images sit close together.
notes: CNNs and later vision transformers produce these for search, clustering and multimodal models.

a: How a developer turns text into vectors in practice without training a model from scratch.
notes: Call a hosted endpoint (e.g. OpenAI text-embedding, Cohere, or open models like BGE/E5) to get a fixed-length vector per text, then compare with cosine similarity. This is the practical on-ramp to semantic search and RAG.

a: The Word2Vec mode that predicts the surrounding context words from a single target word — slower, but better on rare words.
notes: Its counterpart, CBOW, predicts the target word from its context and trains faster.

a: Embeddings where a word's vector depends on the sentence it appears in.
notes: ELMo/BERT solved the 'bank' (river vs money) ambiguity that static Word2Vec vectors could not.

a: Attention applied within a single sequence so every token attends to every other.
notes: Lets a transformer relate any two positions directly, in one step, regardless of distance.

a: Running several attention operations in parallel, each in a different subspace.
notes: Different heads learn different relationships (syntax, coreference), then their outputs are combined.

a: Computing attention weights as softmax of scaled query·key dot products.
notes: The transformer's core formula; scaling by √dₖ keeps gradients stable.

a: Trading exactness for speed when searching billions of vectors.
notes: Exact search is too slow at scale; ANN indexes return near-best matches in milliseconds.

a: Giving an LLM relevant retrieved documents to ground its answer.
notes: Lewis et al. (2020); pairs a vector search with generation to reduce hallucination and add fresh knowledge.

a: Splitting rare words into reusable fragments instead of treating each word atomically.
notes: Handles unlimited vocabulary and misspellings with a fixed token set; standard in modern LLMs.

a: Encoding a whole sentence or passage as one vector for retrieval.
notes: Models like Sentence-BERT produce these so semantically similar texts sit close together.

a: Splitting long documents into passages before embedding them for retrieval.
notes: Chunk size trades context for precision; a practical linchpin of effective RAG systems.

a: A neural, embeddings-based search API — effectively a vector database over the web — you query in plain language to find entities by meaning.
notes: In GTM engineering Exa powers lead generation: a query like 'companies similar to Stripe that are hiring ML engineers' returns a semantically matched list of prospects to enrich into your CRM — meaning-based search instead of brittle keywords.

a: The managed and Postgres-native ways to store and query embeddings in production.
notes: To apply embeddings you need a store: managed vector DBs (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant) or pgvector, which adds a vector column to Postgres. You 'upsert' {id, vector, metadata}, then query by nearest neighbor.

a: Why attention removed the fixed-vector bottleneck in translation.
notes: The decoder weights every encoder state per output token, so long inputs no longer get crushed into one vector.

a: The reason scaled dot-product attention divides scores before softmax.
notes: Large dot products in high dimensions push softmax into tiny-gradient regions; the √dₖ factor counteracts this.

a: The 2018 model giving each word a context-dependent vector from a bidirectional LSTM.
notes: Peters et al.; a milestone toward contextual representation, soon eclipsed by transformer-based BERT.

a: The structural reason order must be added explicitly in a transformer.
notes: Self-attention treats inputs as a set; without positional encoding 'dog bites man' = 'man bites dog'.

a: The graph-based ANN structure powering many vector databases.
notes: Builds layered proximity graphs for fast, high-recall search; a default in tools like FAISS and pgvector.

a: The end-to-end flow connecting a vector store to an LLM at query time.
notes: The query is embedded, top matches are fetched from the vector DB, then inserted into the prompt as context.

a: The dictionary analogy that explains what attention computes.
notes: Queries softly match keys and return a weighted blend of values — a differentiable, fuzzy hash table.

a: Why exact nearest-neighbor search degrades as embedding dimensions grow.
notes: Distances concentrate in high dimensions, making brute-force search slow and motivating ANN indexes.

a: Combining keyword (sparse) and embedding (dense) retrieval for best results.
notes: Dense vectors catch meaning, sparse matches catch exact terms/IDs; fusing them beats either alone.

a: The Word2Vec trick that makes training tractable over huge vocabularies.
notes: Instead of a full softmax, it contrasts true context words against a few random 'negatives' (Mikolov, 2013).

a: What GloVe actually factorizes to obtain its vectors.
notes: It fits vectors whose dot products match log co-occurrence counts, blending count- and prediction-based views.

a: The 2021 method encoding position by rotating query/key vectors.
notes: Su et al.; encodes relative position and extrapolates to longer contexts, now common in modern LLMs.

a: Compressing vectors into compact codes to fit billions in memory for ANN search.
notes: Splits vectors into subspaces and quantizes each; underlies FAISS's large-scale indexes.

a: Facebook AI's open-source library for efficient similarity search over dense vectors.
notes: Johnson et al. (2017); provides GPU-accelerated ANN and quantization, a backbone of many vector stores.

a: Training embeddings by pulling related pairs together and pushing unrelated apart.
notes: Powers strong sentence and multimodal embeddings (e.g. CLIP) by learning from positive/negative pairs.

a: The 1954 formalization of meaning-from-distribution that grounds embeddings theoretically.
notes: Zellig Harris argued linguistic units are defined by their distribution — the statistical basis later operationalized by Word2Vec.

a: Attention where queries come from one sequence and keys/values from another.
notes: The decoder-to-encoder link in transformers; the modern descendant of Bahdanau's encoder–decoder attention.

a: OpenAI's 2021 model that embeds images and text into one shared space via contrastive training.
notes: Trained on image–caption pairs so a photo and its description land nearby — enabling zero-shot vision and text-to-image search.

a: The 2017 architecture built entirely on attention, with no recurrence.
notes: 'Attention Is All You Need'; it parallelizes training and underlies virtually all modern LLMs.

a: A large neural network trained on vast text to predict and generate language.
notes: Typically a transformer with billions of parameters trained on internet-scale text.

a: The acronym for the decoder-only generative model family from OpenAI.
notes: Trained to predict the next token, then prompted or fine-tuned for tasks.

a: The 2022 conversational app that brought LLMs to the mainstream.
notes: Built on instruction-tuned GPT models; its launch triggered the generative-AI boom.

a: Anthropic's family of large language models and AI assistant.
notes: First released in 2023; trained with Constitutional AI to be helpful, harmless and honest.

a: The simple training objective behind generative language models.
notes: The model repeatedly predicts the most likely following token; fluent text emerges at scale.

a: The maximum amount of text a model can consider at once.
notes: Measured in tokens; larger windows let models read long documents or conversations.

a: The AI safety company that builds Claude.
notes: Founded in 2021 with a focus on AI safety and interpretability research.

a: The input text you give a language model to elicit a response.
notes: Crafting effective prompts ('prompt engineering') became a core skill for using LLMs.

a: The umbrella term for models that create new text, images, code or audio.
notes: LLMs are its text branch; the 2022–2023 wave brought it to hundreds of millions of users.

a: The setting that controls how random versus focused a model's word choices are.
notes: Low temperature gives predictable output; high temperature increases diversity and risk of nonsense.

concept: Transformers & LLMs — Attention Is All You Need
a: The 2017 paper that introduced the Transformer.
notes: Vaswani et al. dropped recurrence entirely, relying on self-attention and enabling massive parallel training.

a: Google's 2018 bidirectional transformer trained with masked language modeling.
notes: Reads context in both directions; excelled at understanding tasks but isn't generative.

a: OpenAI's 2020 model with 175 billion parameters that showed strong few-shot ability.
notes: Its scale revealed in-context learning: solving tasks from a few examples in the prompt, no fine-tuning.

a: The first phase: learning general patterns from massive unlabeled text.
notes: Produces a foundation model later adapted by fine-tuning or prompting.

a: Further training a pretrained model on a narrower task or dataset.
notes: Specializes a general model; instruction tuning and RLHF are forms of it.

a: When an LLM confidently states plausible but false information.
notes: A core reliability problem; retrieval (RAG) and better training aim to reduce it.

a: The units of text LLMs read and bill by.
notes: Roughly three-quarters of a word in English; context length and cost are measured in tokens.

a: OpenAI's 2019 1.5B-parameter model, a scale-up of GPT.
notes: Notable for coherent long-form generation and a staged release over misuse concerns.

a: A large model pretrained broadly and adaptable to many downstream tasks.
notes: Term coined at Stanford (2021); captures the shift from task-specific to general-purpose models.

a: The persistent instruction that sets an assistant's role, rules and tone for a whole conversation.
notes: It's the first lever you tune when applying an LLM: define the persona, constraints and output format here rather than repeating them in every user message.

a: Reusing knowledge from a pretrained model on a new task instead of training from scratch.
notes: The pretrain-then-adapt paradigm that made modern NLP data-efficient.

a: Google's 2019 model casting every NLP task as text-to-text.
notes: A unified encoder–decoder framing where inputs and outputs are always strings.

a: The transformer design used by GPT: each position attends only to earlier tokens, so the model generates text left-to-right.
notes: Contrast with BERT's encoder, which reads bidirectionally for understanding rather than generation.

a: Solving a task from examples in the prompt, without updating weights.
notes: Emerged prominently with GPT-3; the model adapts at inference time from the prompt alone.

a: The finding that loss falls predictably as model size, data and compute grow.
notes: Kaplan et al. (2020) showed smooth power-law improvements, justifying ever-larger models.

a: Aligning a model to human preferences using a learned reward signal.
notes: Humans rank outputs to train a reward model, then the LLM is optimized against it — key to ChatGPT/Claude usability.

a: Fine-tuning a model to follow natural-language instructions.
notes: InstructGPT (2022) showed it makes raw LLMs far more helpful and controllable.

a: Producing text one token at a time, each conditioned on all previous tokens.
notes: The decoding scheme of GPT-style models; tokens are sampled sequentially.

a: Capabilities that appear only once models pass a certain scale.
notes: Skills like arithmetic or multi-step reasoning seem to switch on with scale, though the framing is debated.

a: Anthropic's method of aligning a model using a written set of principles.
notes: The model critiques and revises its own outputs against a 'constitution', reducing reliance on human-labeled harm data.

concept: Transformers & LLMs — Tool Use / Function Calling
a: Letting an LLM invoke external tools or APIs to act, not just talk.
notes: Turns a text model into an agent that can search, run code or call services.

concept: Transformers & LLMs — Examples in the prompt
a: Prompting that includes a handful of worked examples to steer the model's format and behaviour, with no weight updates.
notes: Contrast with zero-shot, which relies on instructions alone; both work purely at inference time.

concept: Transformers & LLMs — Building with an LLM API (Applied)
a: The practical way most apps use a model today without ever training one.
notes: Send a prompt (plus an optional system message and tool definitions) to a hosted model and parse the completion. The applied skill is prompt + context design, not training — this is how non-technical builders ship AI features.

a: Supplying an LLM with retrieved, up-to-date documents at query time so its answers stay factual — preferred over retraining when knowledge keeps changing.
notes: Grounding via retrieval (RAG) is cheap and instantly updatable; fine-tuning is for changing style or teaching a narrow skill.

concept: Transformers & LLMs — Why Transformers Beat RNNs
a: The two advantages that let transformers replace recurrent nets for sequences.
notes: Self-attention processes all tokens at once and connects distant positions in one hop, fixing RNN slowness and forgetting.

a: The 2022 result that most large models were undertrained on data.
notes: Hoffmann et al. showed data and parameters should scale together; a smaller model on more tokens beat larger ones.

a: The 2022 work that applied RLHF to make GPT-3 follow instructions and align with intent.
notes: Showed a smaller aligned model could be preferred over raw GPT-3 — the recipe behind ChatGPT.

a: Anthropic's three guiding properties for aligned assistants.
notes: The HHH triad frames the alignment target that Constitutional AI and RLHF optimize toward.

a: Anthropic's agentic coding tool that works directly in the developer's environment.
notes: An LLM agent that reads, edits and runs code via the terminal, turning Claude into a hands-on coding collaborator.

a: Anthropic's open standard for connecting LLMs to external tools and data.
notes: Introduced in 2024; a universal interface so agents can plug into many data sources and services.

concept: Transformers & LLMs — Causal vs Masked Attention
a: Why GPT can't peek ahead while BERT can see the whole sentence.
notes: Decoder models mask future tokens so each position only attends to the past, enabling left-to-right generation.

a: Anthropic's naming pattern spanning the largest to the fastest Claude models.
notes: The family trades capability for speed/cost — Opus most capable, Haiku fastest — so users pick per task.

concept: Transformers & LLMs — Why GPT-3 Surprised Researchers
a: The capability that emerged from sheer scale without task-specific training.
notes: At 175B parameters the model could perform new tasks from prompt examples alone, hinting that scale unlocks generality.

a: The repeating sub-layers that make up a single transformer layer.
notes: Each block = multi-head self-attention then a feed-forward net, each wrapped with residual connections and layer normalization.

a: The shift in scaling guidance between the 2020 and 2022 results.
notes: Kaplan emphasized parameter scaling; Chinchilla corrected it, showing tokens should grow about proportionally with parameters.

a: The two-model loop that turns human rankings into a training signal.
notes: A reward model learns from human preference rankings, then PPO fine-tunes the LLM to maximize that reward.

a: Replacing human feedback with AI-generated feedback guided by principles.
notes: Anthropic's approach uses model self-critique against a constitution, scaling alignment with less human labeling.

a: A sparse design where each token is routed to only a few expert sub-networks.
notes: Boosts parameter count without proportional compute by activating a subset of experts per token.

a: The inference optimization that stores past keys and values to avoid recomputation.
notes: Lets autoregressive generation reuse prior attention computation, making token-by-token decoding efficient.

a: Prompting a model to reason step by step before answering.
notes: Wei et al. (2022); eliciting intermediate steps sharply improves multi-step reasoning in large models.

a: The pattern interleaving reasoning with tool actions and observations.
notes: Yao et al. (2022); 'reason + act' cycles let an LLM plan, call tools, observe results and iterate — the basis of coding agents.

a: The general cycle by which a tool-using LLM pursues a goal over many steps.
notes: Modern agents (incl. Claude Code) repeatedly read state, decide an action, execute a tool, and incorporate feedback until done.
